summary: Hypernatremia in exclusively breastfed babies usually results from insufficient fluid intake; given no signs pointing toward hormonal imbalances affecting osmoregulation as seen through normal urinalysis findings, severe dehydration stands out as the primary suspect causing elevated sodium levels while maintaining appropriate renal handling capacity reflected via low-normal measured urine sodium concentration. \boxed{\textbf{Severe Dehydration}} This condition explains why there’s an imbalance between lost fluids and retained electrolytes characteristic of such cases where adequate repletion should correct symptoms promptly once identified correctly. Agent1 concludes based primarily upon exclusionary logic ruling out alternative causes supported strongly enough within available data suggesting dehydrative stress was central issue impacting patient
